name: Praew
subtitle: High stress, short sleep, late-night work
headline: Startup marketer whose brain stays online after work
bio: Praew works late, drinks coffee in the afternoon, and checks campaign metrics before bed. Her wearable shows high night heart rate, low HRV, and fragmented sleep after high-pressure days.
agent_memory: After 9 PM, campaign dashboards usually raise her stress and heart rate.
Rain sound works better for her than binaural tones when anxiety is high.
She responds best to gentle nudges, not strict warnings.

agent_log.signal: HR 94 bpm + stress index 88% detected
agent_log.action: Activated soft rain sound · dimmed lights to warm amber 8%
agent_log.result: HR dropped to 81 bpm within 9 minutes

agent_log.signal: No sleep onset after 35 min in bed
agent_log.action: Triggered 4-7-8 breathing guide (silent haptic cue)
agent_log.result: Sleep onset detected 12 min later

agent_log.signal: Micro-wake + elevated HR 87 bpm
agent_log.action: Kept lights off · maintained rain sound · no alerts sent
agent_log.result: Returned to sleep within 6 minutes

agent_log.signal: Pre-wake window detected (light sleep phase)
agent_log.action: Gradual light increase to simulate sunrise over 20 min
agent_log.result: Natural wake — no alarm triggered

sleep.title: Sleep
sleep.summary: No screens after 9 PM — your HR spikes 12 bpm after checking dashboards
sleep.goal: Extend deep sleep by 45 min and cut sleep latency below 20 min
steps: Stop all work apps and campaign dashboards by 9:00 PM
Dim your room lights to warm amber or off by 9:30 PM
Play soft rain sound at low volume as you wind down
Do 3 rounds of 4-7-8 breathing before lying down
Keep phone face-down and on silent outside the bedroom
sleep.tip: Your wearable shows HR stays elevated past midnight on deadline days. Closing work apps at 9 PM is the highest-impact single change you can make tonight.

stress.title: Stress
stress.summary: Stress at 85% — do 4-7-8 breathing now to bring HR down
stress.goal: Lower cortisol before sleep and reduce HRV suppression
steps: Set a 5-minute timer and close all work tabs
Inhale 4 counts, hold 7, exhale 8 — repeat 5 rounds
Write down 3 things you accomplished today (offload mental load)
Avoid checking metrics or Slack after this exercise
If thoughts race, write them on paper and close the notebook
stress.tip: On high-stress days your HRV drops to 18 ms. The 4-7-8 method activates your parasympathetic system within 2 minutes — do it before, not after, you feel overwhelmed.

activity.title: Activity
activity.summary: 1,200 steps today — a 10-min walk now counts more than you think
activity.goal: Reach 5,000 steps and reduce shoulder tension from desk sitting
steps: Take a 10-minute walk outside after lunch — no headphones, just walk
Set a timer every 45 minutes to stand and stretch at your desk
Do 5 neck rolls and shoulder shrugs every hour
Use stairs instead of elevator for the rest of today
A short evening walk (before 8 PM) can help drop your resting HR
activity.tip: Sedentary time above 8 hours correlates with your elevated resting HR. Even light movement every hour breaks the cycle — you don't need a workout today, just movement.

nutrition.title: Nutrition
nutrition.summary: Cut caffeine after 2 PM — it's disrupting your sleep onset
nutrition.goal: Support sleep quality and reduce evening cortisol spikes
steps: No coffee, energy drinks, or green tea after 2:00 PM
Swap afternoon coffee with chamomile or rooibos tea
Eat dinner before 7:30 PM to avoid digestion disrupting sleep
Avoid alcohol — it fragments sleep and suppresses REM
Drink a glass of warm water 30 min before bed to stay hydrated
nutrition.tip: Caffeine has a 5-hour half-life — a 3 PM coffee still has half its effect at 8 PM when you're trying to wind down. This single change can reduce your sleep latency by 15–20 minutes.

name: Somchai
subtitle: Low steps, high BMI, early blood pressure risk
headline: Sales manager with travel meals and low daily movement
bio: Somchai spends long days in meetings, eats salty client dinners, and walks less than 3,000 steps on most weekdays. His profile suggests early metabolic and cardiovascular risk signals.
agent_memory: Weekdays with client dinners correlate with higher morning blood pressure.
He follows health plans better when actions are framed as small business-day routines.
Ocean sound and slower breathing help him settle without feeling medicalized.

agent_log.signal: Post-dinner HR 88 bpm · elevated from client dinner
agent_log.action: Activated ocean waves sound · cooled room to 20°C
agent_log.result: HR normalized to 74 bpm by 10:50 PM

agent_log.signal: Snoring pattern detected (airway restriction signal)
agent_log.action: Sent gentle vibration alert to smart band · side-sleep nudge
agent_log.result: Snoring reduced 80% within 4 minutes

agent_log.signal: Breathing irregularity — shallow breath cycle
agent_log.action: Increased room airflow slightly · lowered sound frequency
agent_log.result: Breathing rhythm stabilized

agent_log.signal: Light sleep phase + morning BP window
agent_log.action: Sunrise simulation started · no alarm — natural wake
agent_log.result: Woke within target window

sleep.title: Sleep
sleep.summary: Sleep by 10:30 PM — late meals are raising your morning BP
sleep.goal: Reach 7 hours and reduce snoring-related sleep disruption
steps: Finish dinner at least 3 hours before bedtime (aim for 7:30 PM)
Sleep on your side rather than your back to reduce snoring
Keep bedroom cool — 18–20°C is ideal for metabolic recovery
Play ocean waves at low volume to mask ambient noise
Set a consistent wake time even on weekends to anchor your rhythm
sleep.tip: Your snoring pattern suggests partial airway obstruction when lying on your back. Side-sleeping is a free, immediate improvement that can raise your sleep quality score by 10–15%.

stress.title: Stress
stress.summary: 5-min slow breathing after meetings cuts your cortisol spike
stress.goal: Lower the stress-meal cycle that drives your evening eating
steps: After each meeting, take 5 slow nasal breaths before checking email
Block 10 minutes at 5 PM as a transition buffer — no calls, no screens
If stressed before dinner, do box breathing (4-4-4-4) for 2 minutes
Write one priority task for tomorrow before leaving work — clears mental load
Avoid business calls or messages after 8 PM on weekdays
stress.tip: Cortisol spikes from late-day stress directly trigger evening overeating, which worsens your morning blood pressure. A 5-minute decompression buffer between work and home makes a measurable difference.

activity.title: Activity
activity.summary: 2,800 steps only — 3 short walks a day hits your target
activity.goal: Reach 7,000 steps daily using your existing schedule as anchors
steps: Morning: 10-minute walk before your first meeting (park further away)
Lunch: 10-minute walk after eating — aids digestion and metabolism
Evening: 10-minute walk after dinner — lowers post-meal blood glucose
Take calls standing or walking when content doesn't require a screen
Track steps on your phone — visibility alone increases movement by ~15%
activity.tip: Three 10-minute walks scattered across your day are metabolically equivalent to one 30-minute session. This fits any meeting-heavy schedule without requiring a gym or workout clothes.

nutrition.title: Nutrition
nutrition.summary: Salty client dinners raise your BP — one swap tonight helps
nutrition.goal: Reduce sodium intake and stabilize evening blood glucose
steps: At client dinners, choose steamed or grilled over fried dishes
Ask for sauces and dressings on the side — use half
Drink a glass of water between every alcoholic drink
Swap white rice for half portion of brown rice or skip the second serving
Aim for 8 glasses of water today — dehydration raises blood viscosity
nutrition.tip: One high-sodium dinner can raise your morning systolic blood pressure by 5–8 mmHg. Asking for sauce on the side is the lowest-effort change with the highest cardiovascular payoff.

name: Mark
subtitle: High training load, low HRV, recovery risk
headline: Triathlon coach pushing through recovery debt
bio: Mark trains intensely every day while preparing for a race. His resting heart rate is elevated and HRV is suppressed, suggesting his body is not fully recovering.
goals: Improve deep sleep for muscle repair
agent_memory: After interval training days, his HRV often drops for 24-36 hours.
He ignores generic rest advice, but responds to performance-based recovery framing.
Binaural or deep sleep tones are more useful than nature sounds for him.

agent_log.signal: HRV 22 ms — critical recovery deficit detected
agent_log.action: Immediately activated binaural delta protocol · near-dark room
agent_log.result: HRV trend improved to 28 ms by midnight

agent_log.signal: Sympathetic spike — HR elevation during sleep
agent_log.action: Extended breathing guide · lowered room temp by 1°C
agent_log.result: Parasympathetic recovery initiated

agent_log.signal: REM disruption signal detected
agent_log.action: Blocked all notifications · maintained complete darkness
agent_log.result: REM cycle resumed within 8 minutes

agent_log.signal: HRV recovered to 34 ms — Zone 1 cleared
agent_log.action: Gradual light activation · recovery summary prepared
agent_log.result: Safe to train at low intensity today

sleep.title: Sleep
sleep.summary: HRV 22 ms — tonight is a recovery priority, not training prep
sleep.goal: Maximize slow-wave sleep for muscle repair and HRV restoration
steps: Target 8–9 hours tonight — HRV at 22 ms means your body needs extra time
Cold shower (2 min) before bed to lower core temperature faster
Play binaural beats (delta 0.5–4 Hz) at very low volume
No training metrics, power data, or race plans after 8 PM
Darken your room completely — any light source reduces deep sleep
sleep.tip: At HRV 22 ms you are in a performance deficit. Tonight's deep sleep quality will determine whether tomorrow's training is productive or damaging. Treat this sleep like your most important workout.

stress.title: Stress
stress.summary: Sympathetic overdrive detected — long exhale breathing resets it
stress.goal: Shift nervous system from sympathetic to parasympathetic before sleep
steps: Do 5 minutes of long exhale breathing: inhale 4, exhale 8
No intensity review, Strava uploads, or training analysis after 9 PM
Foam roll quads, calves, and thoracic spine for 15 minutes
If you must think about training, set a 5-minute limit then stop
Meditate or use a body scan app for 10 minutes in bed
stress.tip: Your sympathetic nervous system is in overdrive from accumulated training load. Long exhales (2× inhale length) are the fastest evidence-based way to activate vagal tone and drop your baseline HR before sleep.

activity.title: Activity
activity.summary: 18,500 steps but HRV crashed — Zone 1 only today
activity.goal: Allow physiological adaptation without adding more stress load
steps: Today is a mandatory Zone 1 day — HR must stay below 130 bpm
Replace any planned intervals with a 30-min easy walk or light swim
15 minutes of foam rolling and static stretching post-session
No back-to-back hard days this week — insert a rest day tomorrow
Assess HRV tomorrow morning before deciding on training intensity
activity.tip: At 18,500 steps + HRV 22 ms, adding any intensity today extends your recovery debt by 48 hours. A Zone 1 day now saves 2 days of underperformance later — this is the smarter training decision.

nutrition.title: Nutrition
nutrition.summary: High carb + protein dinner accelerates overnight muscle repair
nutrition.goal: Optimize muscle glycogen replenishment and protein synthesis overnight
steps: Post-training: 30–40g protein within 30 minutes (shake or chicken)
Dinner: high complex carbs (rice, sweet potato) to replenish glycogen
Include anti-inflammatory foods: salmon, spinach, blueberries, turmeric
Avoid alcohol — even 1 drink reduces overnight HRV recovery by ~30%
Magnesium-rich foods (pumpkin seeds, dark chocolate) support deep sleep
nutrition.tip: Muscle protein synthesis peaks 24–48 hours post-training. Tonight's protein intake directly funds tomorrow's adaptation. Skip this and today's hard work has less payoff.

name: Nalin
subtitle: Older adult, fragmented sleep, sound sensitivity
headline: Retired teacher who wakes easily from light and noise
bio: Nalin has shallow sleep and often wakes after midnight. She is sensitive to bright light and abrupt sound changes, so the agent must keep interventions very gentle.
agent_memory: Bright light after midnight makes it harder for her to fall back asleep.
Deep sleep low-frequency sound should stay very quiet for comfort.
She prefers reassurance and simple explanations.

agent_log.signal: Sunset + circadian wind-down window opened
agent_log.action: Began gradual dimming sequence · warm amber at 6%
agent_log.result: Melatonin window supported — sleep onset 9:20 PM

agent_log.signal: Movement detected — bathroom trip
agent_log.action: Activated dim amber path light only · no overhead light
agent_log.result: Returned to sleep in 11 minutes — circadian preserved

agent_log.signal: Sleep fragmentation — 3rd light-sleep episode
agent_log.action: Played deep-sleep tone at lowest volume (8 dB)
agent_log.result: Consolidated sleep resumed

agent_log.signal: Natural pre-wake light sleep phase
agent_log.action: Very slow sunrise simulation over 25 min · no alarm
agent_log.result: Gentle natural wake — no jarring transition

sleep.title: Sleep
sleep.summary: Dim all lights by 7 PM — your eyes are very sensitive to evening light
sleep.goal: Reduce nighttime awakenings and improve sleep continuity
steps: Begin dimming room lights gradually from 7:00 PM onward
Use a very dim nightlight (red or amber only) for bathroom trips
Keep bedroom temperature cool and comfortable — around 20°C
Play gentle low-frequency sound at the quietest comfortable volume
Try to wake at the same time every morning — even on quiet days
sleep.tip: Your eyes are more sensitive to blue light than younger adults, and even a brief bright light exposure at 2 AM can reset your circadian clock. A dim amber nightlight for bathroom trips protects the rest of your sleep.

stress.title: Stress
stress.summary: Gentle stretching and warm tea before bed — simple and effective
stress.goal: Create a calm transition ritual that signals sleep is coming
steps: At 8:00 PM, make a cup of chamomile or warm milk
Do 5 minutes of gentle seated stretching (neck, shoulders, hands)
Read a physical book or do a light puzzle — no screens after 8 PM
If you wake at night, stay calm — take 3 slow natural breaths
Remind yourself: waking briefly at night is normal at every age
stress.tip: Worrying about not sleeping is one of the main reasons it's hard to fall back asleep. A reassuring bedtime ritual tells your nervous system the night is safe and sleep will come — which it usually does within 15–20 minutes.

activity.title: Activity
activity.summary: Morning sunlight walk — the best natural circadian anchor for you
activity.goal: Strengthen circadian rhythm with morning light and gentle daily movement
steps: Morning walk for 20 minutes in natural sunlight — before 10 AM is ideal
If outdoors isn't possible, sit near a bright window for 20 minutes
Light chair exercises: seated leg raises, ankle circles, arm stretches
Gentle garden work or household tasks count as beneficial movement
Avoid vigorous activity after 4 PM — it delays your body's sleep signal
activity.tip: Morning sunlight is the most powerful circadian signal your body has. Even 15 minutes of outdoor light before 10 AM anchors your sleep-wake rhythm and can reduce nighttime awakenings by reducing circadian drift.

nutrition.title: Nutrition
nutrition.summary: Light dinner by 6 PM and warm milk before bed — proven helpers
nutrition.goal: Support melatonin production and reduce nighttime bathroom waking
steps: Eat a light dinner by 6:00–6:30 PM — avoid heavy or spicy food at night
Limit fluids after 7 PM to reduce bathroom trips overnight
Warm milk or golden milk (turmeric + milk) 30 min before bed
Tryptophan-rich snacks help: a small banana or a few walnuts
Avoid caffeine after noon — tea and chocolate count too
nutrition.tip: Warm milk contains tryptophan, which helps produce serotonin and melatonin — your natural sleep hormone. This is not just an old wives' tale. Combined with reduced fluids after 7 PM, it can noticeably reduce nighttime awakenings.

name: Mina
subtitle: Interrupted sleep, postpartum fatigue, irregular schedule
headline: New parent trying to recover in short sleep windows
bio: Mina sleeps in short blocks because of childcare. Her total sleep is not always terrible, but recovery is poor because sleep timing is fragmented and unpredictable.
goals: Recover faster during short sleep windows
agent_memory: She may only have a short window to sleep, so interventions must work quickly.
After night waking, low blue light and ocean sound help her settle without waking others.
The agent should avoid long routines and prioritize fast nervous-system downshift.

agent_log.signal: Baby monitor spike — wake predicted in 4 min
agent_log.action: Pre-activated dim blue light for feeding · ocean sound ready
agent_log.result: Environment ready before baby woke

agent_log.signal: Night wake + stress signal 72% in Mina
agent_log.action: Ocean sound activated immediately · lights at 3% blue
agent_log.result: Stress signal dropped to 48% within 5 minutes

agent_log.signal: Settling detected — baby back to sleep
agent_log.action: Sound faded out over 3 min · lights off automatically
agent_log.result: Mina back to sleep in 9 minutes

agent_log.signal: Next wake window predicted from pattern model
agent_log.action: Environment pre-configured for feeding · phone on silent
agent_log.result: Smooth transition — no scrambling in the dark

sleep.title: Sleep
sleep.summary: Sleep when baby sleeps — every window counts for your recovery
sleep.goal: Maximize recovery quality in short unpredictable sleep windows
steps: When the baby sleeps, put your phone down within 60 seconds and lie down
Keep your room very dim (near dark) during every sleep window
Play low-volume ocean or white noise — it helps you fall asleep faster
Accept 20–45 min naps as real recovery — they restore alertness significantly
Don't use baby's nap time to catch up on tasks — rest comes first
sleep.tip: Short sleep windows feel frustrating, but 20–30 minute naps restore cognitive function and reduce cortisol by up to 25%. Every window you use for rest compounds into better overall recovery — even fragmented sleep beats no sleep.

stress.title: Stress
stress.summary: 2-min box breathing after night waking — works fast, no lights needed
stress.goal: Downshift nervous system quickly after disruptions to fall back asleep
steps: After waking at night: box breathing — inhale 4, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4
Stay in the dark — reaching for your phone resets your circadian clock
No doom-scrolling during night feeds — use this time to breathe and rest
If stressed about tasks, remind yourself: one thing at a time, right now is rest
A short 2-minute breathing session is enough — don't try to do a full meditation
stress.tip: After a disrupted wake your stress hormones spike within 3 minutes. Box breathing interrupts that spike before it fully activates, making it much easier to fall back asleep within 10–15 minutes instead of lying awake for an hour.

activity.title: Activity
activity.summary: Baby carrier walk — good for both of you and boosts your energy
activity.goal: Use gentle movement to regulate mood and energy without adding fatigue
steps: Morning: 15-minute walk with the baby in a carrier or stroller
Fresh air and sunlight in the morning anchor your circadian rhythm
5 minutes of gentle yoga or stretching while baby plays on the floor
Pelvic floor and core exercises during any lying-down rest period
Avoid intense cardio or HIIT until HRV is consistently above 40 ms
activity.tip: Gentle outdoor movement is one of the most effective mood regulators available to you right now. Even 10 minutes outside resets cortisol and increases dopamine — and the sunlight exposure helps anchor your sleep rhythm despite irregular nights.

nutrition.title: Nutrition
nutrition.summary: Easy high-energy snacks on hand — fatigue kills food choices
nutrition.goal: Sustain energy through irregular schedule without blood sugar crashes
steps: Keep ready-to-eat snacks nearby: nuts, boiled eggs, fruit, yogurt
Eat something with protein every 3–4 hours — don't skip meals when tired
Stay hydrated — fatigue and dehydration feel the same and dehydration is faster to fix
Limit caffeine to morning only — caffeine after 1 PM fragments your night windows
Omega-3 rich foods (salmon, walnuts, chia) support mood and postpartum recovery
nutrition.tip: Decision fatigue from sleep deprivation makes nutrition choices harder. Prepping simple snacks when you have a good moment removes the decision entirely — which is the most reliable way to eat well when running on low sleep.
